positional_bias.note: All four positions used; max_share 0.333 is within the 0.40 ceiling.

per_question.note: Correct option (pos 3) is 166 chars vs distractor mean 128.7; exceeds 1.20x threshold of 154.4. Flagged but rate (1/6=0.167) is well below the 0.40 fail threshold.

distractor_plausibility.scoring_notes: All 18 distractors score 3 or 4 on the 1-4 plausibility scale. Each distractor represents a plausible beginner misconception: confusing LLM internals with agent components (q1), overestimating prompt engineering (q2), fixed-loop mental model (q3), in-context learning confusion (q4), prompting techniques as tools (q5), over-engineering instinct (q6).

lo_coverage.lo: LO1 — Name the four components of an AI agent and describe what each does.
LO2 — Explain why an LLM on its own is not an agent, and what the other three components add.
LO3 — Describe what the planning loop does and why it is necessary for multi-step tasks.
LO4 — Distinguish between short-term memory and long-term memory in the agent context.
LO5 — Explain what a tool is in the agent context and give two concrete examples.
LO6 — Identify the kinds of tasks where an agent is the right choice vs a simpler approach.

beginner_fairness_and_scope.notes: All stems are in plain language. 'System prompt' referenced in q2 distractors is covered by earlier prompt-engineering topics in the sequence. 'Vector database' appears in a q4 distractor; topic 14.1 precedes 14.5 in week 14, making this an in-scope reference. No forward references to topics 14.6+. Acronym 'LLM' is used in stems but was defined in earlier curriculum topics; reuse without re-definition is permitted per artifact-mix audience rules.

verdict: Pass — all five checks clean. Positional max_share 0.333, length-bias rate 0.167 (1/6, q3 flagged but sub-threshold), zero absurd distractors, all 6 LOs covered 1:1, no out-of-scope questions.
